title: Properties of Operations

projects.title: Unit 6 Project — Expressions in Action
projects.desc: Capstone: apply exponents, expressions, and the distributive property to a real-world project.

projects.title: Distributive Alchemy
projects.desc: Use the distributive property to transform and expand expressions.

projects.title: Equivalent Expressions Forge
projects.desc: Generate and verify equivalent algebraic expressions.

contentObjective: I can use the commutative, associative, and identity properties to rewrite expressions.
languageObjective: I can explain my reasoning using the words commutative property, associative property, and identity property.

turnAndTalk.question: The band has 3 guitarists, 5 drummers, and 2 keyboard players. Whether you seat guitarists or drummers first, you still have 10 musicians. Which property explains this, and why does it work?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The commutative property lets you change the order of a sum without changing the total.

stems.en: Changing the order still gives 10 because of the ___ property.
stems.es: Cambiar el orden todavía da 10 por la propiedad ___.

stems.en: It works because order does not change ___.
stems.es: Funciona porque el orden no cambia ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students name the commutative property and explain that 3 + 5 + 2 gives 10 in any order because reordering addends does not change the sum.
turnAndTalk.extend: Would this same reordering trick work if you were subtracting musicians who left the stage? Justify why the commutative property fails for subtraction.

turnAndTalk.question: How can you tell the commutative property from the associative property? Use 6 + 9 = 9 + 6 and (3 + 5) + 2 = 3 + (5 + 2) to explain.
turnAndTalk.kernel: Commutative changes the order; associative changes the grouping.

stems.en: The ___ property changes the order, like 6 + 9 = 9 + 6.
stems.es: La propiedad ___ cambia el orden, como 6 + 9 = 9 + 6.

stems.en: The ___ property changes the grouping because ___.
stems.es: La propiedad ___ cambia la agrupación porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer connects commutative to changed order and associative to changed parentheses (grouping), noting both keep the same value.
turnAndTalk.extend: Explain why the identity property (like 14 + 0 = 14) is a different idea from both. Generalize what each of the three properties keeps the same.
extendStems: The identity property is different because it ___ rather than reorder or regroup.
All three keep the value the same, but they change ___, ___, or ___.

turnAndTalk.question: To find 4 × 17 × 25 mentally, the engineer rearranges to 4 × 25 × 17 = 100 × 17. Which properties did she use, and why does rearranging help?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Use commutative and associative properties to make a friendly factor of 100.

stems.en: She used the ___ property to change the order and the ___ property to regroup.
stems.es: Usó la propiedad ___ para cambiar el orden y la propiedad ___ para reagrupar.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students name both the commutative and associative properties, compute 4 × 25 = 100, and explain pairing to a friendly number makes the mental math easier (100 × 17 = 1700).
turnAndTalk.extend: Could you use the same strategy to compute 2 × 13 × 50 quickly? Justify which numbers you would pair and why.
extendStems: I would pair ___ and ___ because their product is ___.
Rearranging works here because the properties let me ___ without changing the answer.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Properties of Operations, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer.

stems.en: The ___ property changes the order, like 6 + 9 = 9 + 6.
stems.es: La propiedad ___ cambia el orden, como 6 + 9 = 9 + 6.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.EE.3 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

vocabulary.definition: You can change the order and get the same answer.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Puedes cambiar el orden y obtener la misma respuesta.
vocabulary.visual: 3 + 7 = 7 + 3 = 10 both ways; 4 × 5 = 5 × 4 = 20 both ways — order does not matter
vocabulary.cloze: The rule that order does not change a sum or product, like a + b = b + a, is the ___.

examples.why: Changing the order keeps the same sum.
That shows the associative property, which regroups.

vocabulary.definitionVi: Thứ tự của các số trong phép cộng hoặc nhân không thay đổi kết quả.
vocabulary.definitionAr: ترتيب الأرقام في الجمع أو الضرب لا يغير النتيجة.

vocabulary.definition: You can change the grouping and get the same answer.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Puedes cambiar la agrupación y obtener la misma respuesta.
vocabulary.cloze: The rule that grouping does not change a sum or product, like (a + b) + c = a + (b + c), is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Cách nhóm các số trong phép cộng hoặc phép nhân không làm thay đổi kết quả.
vocabulary.definitionAr: طريقة تجميع الأرقام في الجمع أو الضرب لا تغير النتيجة.

vocabulary.definition: Adding 0 or multiplying by 1 keeps the same value.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Sumar 0 o multiplicar por 1 mantiene el mismo valor.
vocabulary.visual: 9 + 0 = 9 (zero is the identity for addition); 6 × 1 = 6 (one is the identity for multiplication)
vocabulary.cloze: The rule that adding 0 or multiplying by 1 keeps a number the same is the ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Phép toán với 0 (phép cộng) hoặc 1 (phép nhân) giữ nguyên giá trị ban đầu.
vocabulary.definitionAr: العملية مع الصفر (في الجمع) أو الواحد (في الضرب) تحافظ على الرقم الأصلي.

vocabulary.definition: A rule that is always true in math.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una regla que siempre es verdadera en matemáticas.
vocabulary.visual: Commutative works for + and ×, but NOT for − or ÷ (since 5 − 3 = 2 but 3 − 5 = −2)
vocabulary.cloze: A rule that is always true in math is a ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Một quy tắc hoặc đặc điểm toán học luôn đúng.
vocabulary.definitionAr: قاعدة أو سمة رياضية صحيحة دائماً.

vocabulary.definition: The number in front of a letter, like the 3 in 3x.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El número frente a una letra, como el 3 en 3x.
vocabulary.visual: In 3x, the 3 is the coefficient — it tells you to multiply x by 3
vocabulary.cloze: A number multiplied by a variable is a ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Số được nhân với biến trong một số hạng đại số.
vocabulary.definitionAr: الرقم الذي يتم ضربه بالمتغير في الحد الجبري.

vocabulary.definition: Terms with the same letter, like 2x and 5x.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Términos con la misma letra, como 2x y 5x.
vocabulary.visual: 4x + 2x = 6x (like terms, both have x); 4x + 2y stays as 4x + 2y (unlike terms)
vocabulary.cloze: Terms with the same variable part are ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Các số hạng có cùng biến số và số mũ.
vocabulary.definitionAr: الحدود التي تحتوي على نفس المتغيرات والأسس.

launch.narrative: You're a band director arranging musicians on stage. You have 3 guitarists, 5 drummers, and 2 keyboard players. Whether you seat the guitarists first or the drummers first, you still have 3 + 5 + 2 = 10 musicians. The order you arrange them doesn't change the total!
launch.contextImage: Music studio stage with musicians being rearranged in different formations
noticePrompts: Does the order you add the musicians change the total?
What if you group different sections together first — does the total change?
What happens when you add 0 musicians to a group?
wonderPrompts: Do these same rules work for subtraction or division?
Can rearranging terms help make mental math easier?

conceptIntro.heading: What are the properties of operations?
conceptIntro.intro: Properties are math rules that are always true. The Commutative Property lets you change the order. The Associative Property lets you change the grouping (the parentheses).
conceptIntro.keyIdea: Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: I have 3 guitarists, 5 drummers, and 2 keyboard players.
I can add them as 3 + 5 + 2, which gives 10.
The Commutative Property says order does not matter: 5 + 3 + 2 also gives 10.
The Associative Property says grouping does not matter: (3 + 5) + 2 = 3 + (5 + 2), both equal 10.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Look at 6 + 9 and 9 + 6. Do they give the same total?
Only the order changed, so this shows the Commutative Property.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Sort each example into the property it shows: commutative or associative.
Ask: did the order change, or did the grouping change?

explore.instructions: Read each pair of expressions. Identify which property of operations is being shown.

discourse.prompt: How can you tell the difference between the commutative and associative properties? What clue helps you decide?
discourse.sentenceFrame: The ___ property changes the ___, while the ___ property changes the ___. I can tell by looking at whether the ___ moved or the ___ changed.

optional.instructions: Drag each track on the playlist into the property of operations it shows. Ask yourself: did the order change, the grouping change, or did a 0 or 1 keep the value the same?

optional.stem: Track 2 on the playlist plays 5(7 + 2) = 5 × 7 + 5 × 2. Which property is this?
optional.explanation: Multiplying 5 across both numbers inside the parentheses (5 × 7 and 5 × 2) shows the Distributive Property.

optional.stem: Track 3 plays (7 + 3) + 5 = 7 + (3 + 5). Only the parentheses moved. Which property is this?
optional.explanation: The grouping (parentheses) changed but the order of the numbers stayed the same, so this is the Associative Property.

optional.stem: Track 4 plays 1 × y = y. Which property is this?
optional.explanation: Multiplying by 1 keeps the same value, so this is the Identity Property of Multiplication.

optional.label: Match each playlist track to the property it plays.

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — Catch the Mislabeled Track

workedExample.label: Reasoning
workedExample.work: There are parentheses, so the grouping must have changed.

optional.correctWork: This track actually shows the Distributive Property, not the Associative Property. The 3 is multiplied across both terms inside the parentheses: 3 × a and 3 × b. Check with numbers a = 2, b = 6: 3(2 + 6) = 3 × 8 = 24, and 3 × 2 + 3 × 6 = 6 + 18 = 24. The Associative Property only changes grouping inside one operation, like (a + b) + c = a + (b + c). Distributive spreads a multiplier over a sum.
hints: Did the 3 get multiplied by each number inside the parentheses, or did only the grouping of one operation change?
Test it with a = 2 and b = 6 to see what the property is really doing.

approaching.explanation: The order of the addends changed, so this is the Commutative Property of Addition.
The grouping (parentheses) changed but the order stayed the same, so this is the Associative Property of Multiplication.
Adding 0 does not change the value, so this is the Identity Property of Addition.

approaching.stem: Which property lets you rearrange 8 × 5 to 5 × 8?
approaching.explanation: Changing the order of factors is the Commutative Property of Multiplication.

approaching.instructions: Sort each equation by the property it shows.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Properties of Operations.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer.

onLevel.instructions: Match each equation with the property it demonstrates.

onLevel.label: Match each equation to its property.

onLevel.instructions: Use properties to rewrite each expression in a way that makes mental math easier. Then calculate.
Do both sides equal the same value? Identify which property proves it. Left: (3 + 8) + 2. Right: 3 + (8 + 2).
onLevel.label: Associative Property of Addition: same value (13), different grouping

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Properties of Operations.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer.

extending.title: Find the Property Error

workedExample.label: Student's claim
workedExample.work: This shows the Commutative Property of Subtraction.

workedExample.label: Reasoning
workedExample.work: I switched the order, so it must be commutative.

extending.correctWork: 15 − 8 = 7, but 8 − 15 = −7. These are NOT equal. The commutative property does NOT apply to subtraction. It only works for addition and multiplication.
hints: Does switching the order in subtraction always give the same answer?
Try computing both sides: Is 15 − 8 the same as 8 − 15?

extending.prompt: Explain why the commutative property works for addition and multiplication but NOT for subtraction and division. Give a specific number example for each operation to prove your point.
extending.sentenceFrame: Addition: ___ + ___ = ___ + ___ = ___ (works). Subtraction: ___ − ___ ≠ ___ − ___ because ___. Multiplication: ___ × ___ = ___ × ___ = ___ (works). Division: ___ ÷ ___ ≠ ___ ÷ ___ because ___.

extending.instructions: For each expression, use the commutative and/or associative properties to rearrange and simplify the mental math. Show which property you used.

optionalActivity.name: Property Playlist Sort
optionalActivity.intro: Sort each track on the studio playlist into the property of operations it plays, then catch a mislabeled mix.

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Properties of Operations is skipping the key idea: "Changing the order or the grouping of numbers being added or multiplied does not change the answer." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: A sound engineer needs to calculate 4 × 17 × 25 mentally to find the total cost of audio cables. Using the associative and commutative properties, she rearranges: 4 × 25 × 17 = 100 × 17.
connect.promptQuestion: What properties did the engineer use, and why does rearranging make the calculation easier?
connect.prompt: The engineer used the ___ property to change the ___ and the ___ property to change the ___. This makes it easier because 4 × 25 = ___, which is a friendly number to multiply by.

exitTicket.explanation: The grouping (parentheses) changed but the numbers stayed in the same order, so this is the Associative Property of Addition.

printables.name: Property Sorting Court
printables.desc: Students sort examples by property and justify each with a written explanation.

printables.name: Properties of Operations — Color by Number
printables.desc: Solve each problem, then color by the answer to reveal a picture.
